figures.caption: Figure 11: Training curves on continuous tasks. Each row reports reward, goal distance, and architecture entropy for one task. Higher reward and lower goal distance indicate better performance.

Table 1: Rewards (mean ± std) on discrete benchmark tasks across three runs; higher is better. πcont.-PRL, πdisc.-PRL, and π-PRL denote the relaxed policy before discretization, the discretized policy before fine-tuning, and the final fine-tuned policy, respectively. The best performance among policies (DTSemNets, π-PRL and DiPRL) without oracle is bold for each tasks.

Table 5: Ablation of the regularization coefficient α on discrete-control tasks. In the Discretization column, × denotes the relaxed policy before extraction and ✓ denotes the extracted discrete program. No fine-tuning is applied.
Table 2: Rewards (mean ± std) on the continuous tasks across three runs; higher is better.
Table 7: Final normalized architecture entropy before discretization. Values are mean ± std across three seeds. Lower values indicate that the relaxed architecture is closer to a discrete program.
Table 6: Final discrete program depth (avg. ± std) for π-PRL and DiPRL.
Table 8: Goal distance curves in continuous tasks; smaller is better.

Figure 10: Training curves on discrete tasks. The left column reports reward, where higher is better, and the right column reports architecture entropy. DiPRL reduces architecture entropy during training while maintaining competitive reward.

Figure 4: Workflow of DiPRL. E denotes a program node. A denotes an action node, marked in yellow. B denotes a condition in an incomplete program if B then A else E. The maximal depth Dm is set to 2 in the figure. A programmatic policy is relaxed into a continuous derivation program tree by fully expanding and assigning expansion probabilities to program branches. During the training, the program architecture entropy H(T ) decreases and encourages the relaxed architecture to approach a discrete program.
